findings.summary: time module imported inline inside every method body instead of once at module level
evidence: Line 15: `import time` exists at module top level
Lines 181, 291, 371, 430, 506, 790: `import time as time_module` repeated inside 6 separate method bodies
The module-level import is already present; the inline re-imports shadow it under a different alias and serve no purpose
findings.suggestion: Remove the inline `import time as time_module` statements and alias once at module level with `import time as time_module` replacing `import time`, then use `time_module` consistently.

findings.summary: `prompt_preview` computed but never consumed in `query()` method
evidence: Line 185: `prompt_preview = prompt[:100] + '...' if len(prompt) > 100 else prompt`
Lines 210-215: the subsequent `logger.info('Starting query', extra={...})` dict includes only `model` and `prompt_length` — `prompt_preview` is absent
Same variable is correctly used in `query_raw` (line 302) and `stream_query` (line 804), but the dead assignment in `query()` is a copy-paste residue
findings.suggestion: Remove the `prompt_preview` assignment from `query()` since it is never referenced in that method.

findings.summary: Generated submission checklist and announcement strings are dense with section-emoji decoration that adds no programmatic value
evidence: Lines 92-164: `create_submission_checklist` returns a multi-section markdown string where every heading is prefixed with a decorative emoji (📋, 📄, 🎯, 🔗, 📊, 🧪, 📝, ✅, 📅)
Lines 199-236: `create_publication_announcement` similarly prefixes every heading (🚀, 📝, 🔑, 📊, 🔗, 📚)
Emoji prefixes in machine-generated utility text are a consistent AI hallmark; they do not aid rendering or parsing
findings.suggestion: Strip decorative emoji from generated section headings in `create_submission_checklist` and `create_publication_announcement`; keep only semantic content.

findings.summary: Every LLMClient query method logs both entry and exit with structured extras, creating excessive noise for a synchronous library call
evidence: Each of `query`, `query_raw`, `query_short`, `query_long`, `query_structured`, and `stream_query` emits an INFO-level 'Starting ...' log and a 'Completed ...' log with generation time, response length, and token estimates
`query_short` (lines 376-408) and `query_long` (lines 435-468) duplicate this pattern on top of `query()`, which already logs — a single call to `query_short` produces 4 log lines at INFO
This matches the 'nosy debug logging: entry/exit logs on every function' AI-debt pattern
findings.suggestion: Move per-call timing and token metrics to DEBUG level in `query_short`/`query_long`; let `query()` own the single INFO-level completion record.

findings.summary: `validate_markdown_cli.py` re-implements `find_markdown_files`, `collect_symbols`, `validate_images`, `validate_refs`, and `validate_math` as local helpers that duplicate logic already in `infrastructure/validation/markdown_validator.py`
evidence: Lines 56-82: `find_markdown_files()` re-implements directory scanning with custom sort logic
Lines 85-107: `collect_symbols()` re-implements label/anchor extraction with `re.finditer` patterns already present in the validator
Lines 110-133, 136-159, 162-182: `validate_images`, `validate_refs`, `validate_math` re-implement checks that the imported `validate_markdown` function performs internally
Module comment (line 45) says 'Re-export functions from infrastructure for tests' but the functions are local reimplementations, not re-exports
findings.suggestion: Expose the needed primitives from `infrastructure/validation/markdown_validator.py` directly and import them in the CLI; remove the local reimplementations.

findings.summary: `TemplateError` and all subclasses accept an open `dict[str, Any]` context bag, creating an implicit untyped parameter contract at every raise site
evidence: Line 46: `context: Optional[dict[str, Any]] = None` — completely open bag
82 `config_bag_mentions` counted in exceptions.py by the scanner, indicating wide downstream usage

Different callers stuff heterogeneous keys (`file`, `error`, `script`, `exit_code`, `response`, `url`, `model`) into the same field with no schema enforcement; callers must read source to know what to expect
findings.suggestion: Define per-exception typed context dataclasses (e.g., `FileErrorContext`, `LLMErrorContext`) or use keyword-only constructor params for the most commonly used fields, reserving the dict bag for truly open-ended extensions.

findings.summary: `scripts/02_run_analysis.py` does not use the `get_logger`/`log_header`/`log_success` trio that 15 of 18 sibling scripts use
evidence: Sibling behavior scan reports `get_logger` in 15/18 scripts, `log_header` in 13/18, `log_success` in 11/18
`scripts/02_run_analysis.py` is listed as missing `get_logger`, `log_header`, and `log_success`
The file uses `logger = get_logger(__name__)` at line 49 but then never calls `log_header` or `log_success` while the surrounding stage scripts (01, 03, 04, 05) all do
findings.suggestion: Add `log_header` call at stage entry and `log_success` at stage completion to match the established convention in sibling orchestrators.

findings.summary: `validate_markdown_cli.py` mixes `os.path` / `os.listdir` / `os.path.join` with `pathlib.Path` while every sibling validator uses only `pathlib`
evidence: Lines 65-82: `find_markdown_files` uses `os.listdir`, `os.path.join`, `os.path.basename`, `filter(str.isdigit, ...)` — raw os.path string manipulation
Lines 122-131: `validate_images` uses `os.path.join`, `os.path.dirname`, `os.path.exists`
Siblings `check_links.py`, `integrity.py`, `markdown_validator.py`, `doc_scanner.py` all use `pathlib.Path` exclusively
The module already imports `Path` at line 19 but does not use it in these local helpers
findings.suggestion: Replace `os.path` / `os.listdir` usage in local helpers with `pathlib.Path` methods consistent with sibling modules.

findings.summary: `scripts/__init__.py` is the sole scripts-package file missing `Path`, `sys`, `get_logger`, `log_header`, and `log_success`
evidence: Sibling behavior scan lists it as missing all five shared patterns that 13-17 of its 18 siblings use
The file is a config/metadata module (defines `PipelineStageDefinition` dataclass and `MENU_SCRIPT_MAPPING`) — a genuine style island compared to the orchestrator siblings
findings.suggestion: This is a legitimate config-only module; annotate it with a comment distinguishing it from orchestrator scripts so the sibling outlier is intentional and documented rather than accidental drift.

findings.summary: `requests` is a heavy production dependency used exclusively for LLM (Ollama) HTTP calls, duplicating what the optional `ollama` SDK already handles
evidence: `requests>=2.31.0` is a mandatory production dependency in `pyproject.toml` (line 43)
`ollama>=0.6.1` is also present but in the optional `llm` dependency group (line 61)
`infrastructure/llm/core/client.py` line 21: `import requests` and uses it exclusively for POST to `{base_url}/api/chat` and streaming
The `ollama` Python SDK provides a typed client that handles the same HTTP communication without needing raw `requests`; having both for the same purpose duplicates the HTTP stack
findings.suggestion: Move `requests` to the `llm` optional dependency group, or migrate `LLMClient` to use the `ollama` SDK's client and remove the raw `requests` calls. If raw HTTP is intentional for portability, document why the `ollama` SDK is insufficient.

findings.summary: infrastructure/core/environment.py has 14 top-level functions across 11 disconnected clusters with no unifying abstraction
evidence: Functions span at least four distinct concerns: Python/package checks (check_python_version, check_dependencies, install_missing_packages), build tools (check_build_tools), directory structure (setup_directories, verify_source_structure, validate_directory_structure), uv/subprocess integration (check_uv_available, get_python_command, get_subprocess_env, validate_interpreter, validate_uv_sync_result), and environment variables (set_environment_variables)
Existing findings confirm: 'Mixed responsibilities: 14 top-level funcs across 11 disconnected clusters'
558 LOC with complexity score 57 — too large for a module whose name implies a single concern
findings.suggestion: Split into at minimum three focused modules: dependency_check.py (package/tool availability), directory_setup.py (directory creation/validation), and subprocess_env.py (uv integration, subprocess env, interpreter validation). Each cluster then has a clear ownership boundary.

findings.summary: infrastructure/publishing/core.py contains no error handling on any I/O boundary, silently returning partial results on failure
evidence: create_publication_package iterates pdf_dir.glob and calculate_file_hash but neither branch raises nor catches — if file_path is missing between glob and hash the result is silently incomplete
validate_publication_readiness calls verify_academic_standards and extract_citations_from_markdown with no exception handling; a single unreadable file causes silent incorrect scoring
No try/except anywhere in the module despite two functions that do filesystem I/O
findings.suggestion: Wrap filesystem operations in try/except and either raise a typed PublishingError or return an explicit error indicator so callers know results are partial.

findings.summary: infrastructure/reporting/__init__.py re-exports 18 symbols from 6 internal submodules, creating a wide flat surface that obscures module boundaries
evidence: Imports and re-exports from dashboard_generator, error_aggregator, executive_reporter, output_reporter, pipeline_reporter, output_organizer, and coverage_reporter in a single __init__

coverage_reporter's generate_test_report is aliased as generate_test_report_from_results to avoid name collision with pipeline_reporter's generate_test_report — a naming collision that signals the flat surface is too wide
The collision alias (line 47-48) is a symptom of mixing unrelated reporting subdomains in one namespace
findings.suggestion: Narrow __init__ to only the symbols that are genuinely cross-cutting (e.g., ErrorAggregator, generate_pipeline_report). Have callers import directly from submodules for domain-specific reporters. Remove the alias workaround by not re-exporting both at the same level.

findings.summary: Core infrastructure functions mix three incompatible error strategies — raise, return None, and return bool — without a consistent rule
evidence: calculate_file_hash (file_operations.py:557) raises FileOperationError on PermissionError but returns None on OSError — two different signals for 'could not hash the file'

environment.py functions return bool (check_python_version, check_build_tools, set_environment_variables) while validate_uv_sync_result returns Tuple[bool, str] — callers must know which pattern each function uses

verify_data_consistency (integrity.py) returns Dict[str, bool] for per-file status but verify_file_integrity also returns Dict[str, bool]; verify_output_integrity wraps both yet adds issues/warnings as side-channel lists on the IntegrityReport object — three layers of status reporting for the same concern
holistic_context error strategies for core/: try_catch=163, throws=121, returns_null=28 all coexist with no documented rule for which to use
findings.suggestion: Adopt a layered rule: infrastructure library functions raise typed exceptions (FileOperationError, ValidationError) on failure; CLI/script entry points convert to bool/int exit codes. Eliminate the bool-return pattern from library functions so callers don't need to check both exception and return value.

findings.summary: Several modules use bare except: or except Exception without re-raising, permanently discarding exception context
evidence: check_links.py line 358: `except Exception: return None` — exception type, message, and traceback all lost; caller gets None and has no way to distinguish a logic error from a missing file
repo_scanner.py: existing findings show 5x 'Except handler silently suppresses error (pass/continue, no log)' and 5x empty except blocks
environment.py:492 catches Exception and returns False — the error is logged but not propagated; callers that check only the return value see a bool False with no way to get the underlying cause
findings.suggestion: Replace bare-swallow patterns with: (a) raise ... from err when re-raising, (b) log and re-raise when the caller needs to handle it, or (c) an explicit Optional return with a documented None-means-failed contract. Never silently pass without at minimum a debug log.

findings.summary: Two parallel exception systems exist (TemplateError hierarchy in exceptions.py and InfraError constants in errors.py) with no documented rule for which to use where
evidence: exceptions.py defines TemplateError with 46 importers — a full class hierarchy used for raise statements
errors.py defines InfraError frozen dataclass constants (PIPELINE_STAGE_FAILED, STAGE_FAILED, etc.) used for logger.error(err.format(...)) calls — a message-template system, not an exception system

pipeline.py imports from both: TemplateError-derived exceptions AND InfraError constants; a function that fails may either raise ConfigurationError or call logger.error(PIPELINE_STAGE_FAILED.format(...)) and return False
No module-level comment or ARCHITECTURE note explains the intended split
findings.suggestion: Document the contract: InfraError constants are for human-readable log messages only; TemplateError subclasses are for raising. Audit sites that call logger.error(err.format(...)) and return False — these should raise if the condition is truly exceptional, not just log.

findings.summary: infrastructure/validation/ contains 22 files with mixed concerns in a single flat directory
evidence: 22 files in infrastructure/validation/: audit_orchestrator, check_links, cli, doc_accuracy, doc_completeness, doc_discovery, doc_models, doc_quality, doc_scanner, figure_validator, integrity, issue_categorizer, known_exceptions, link_validator, markdown_validator, mock_validator, output_validator, pdf_validator, repo_scanner, validate_markdown_cli, validate_pdf_cli
At least four distinct sub-concerns are mixed: document scanning, link validation, format validation, integrity/audit
7771 total LOC in one directory; avg fan-in 4.5 but top file (validation/__init__.py) has 24 importers — the flat surface is load-bearing but opaque
findings.suggestion: Reorganize into sub-packages: validation/documents/ (doc_* files), validation/links/ (check_links, link_validator), validation/formats/ (markdown_validator, pdf_validator, cli files), validation/integrity/ (integrity, audit_orchestrator, issue_categorizer, output_validator, figure_validator). The __init__.py can continue to re-export a curated public surface.

findings.summary: infrastructure/validation/mock_validator.py enforces a test policy but lives in a production validation package
evidence: mock_validator.py scans test files for mock framework usage — this is a developer policy enforcement tool, not a production validation concern
scripts/verify_no_mocks.py exists as a standalone script for the same purpose, implying the functionality is duplicated across package boundaries
The file is missing get_logger (flagged in conventions outliers) — consistent with it not following validation module patterns
findings.suggestion: Move mock_validator.py to infrastructure/core/ or a dedicated infrastructure/policy/ subpackage. Consolidate with scripts/verify_no_mocks.py to eliminate duplication.

findings.summary: infrastructure/reporting/ has 12 files (8580 LOC) with at least three distinct reporting sub-concerns in one flat directory
evidence: Files span: pipeline execution reporting (pipeline_reporter, error_aggregator, coverage_parser, coverage_reporter), HTML/visual output (dashboard_generator, html_templates), executive summaries (executive_reporter), output management (output_organizer, output_reporter, manuscript_overview), and summary utilities (summary_generator)
dashboard_generator alone is 3454 LOC with complexity 266 — it has dominated the directory's total LOC count
Existing findings confirm dashboard_generator has 'Mixed responsibilities: 24 top-level funcs across 9 disconnected clusters'
findings.suggestion: Extract reporting/dashboards/ for dashboard_generator and html_templates, reporting/pipeline/ for pipeline_reporter and error_aggregator, reporting/executive/ for executive_reporter. This maps directories to change boundaries.

findings.summary: projects/cognitive_case_diagrams/src/visualization/styles.py has 3 importers and zero dedicated test coverage
evidence: holistic_context.testing.critical_untested lists styles.py with importers=3 as the sole identified critical untested file
styles.py is imported by category_diagram.py, string_diagram.py, and __init__.py — all visualization entry points depend on its constants and color mappings

No test file exercises get_case_color, get_wire_color, or the DiagramTheme enum directly; the tests that import CASE_COLORS (test_edge_cases.py, test_package_init.py) only use the constant as fixture data, not as the subject under test
findings.suggestion: Add tests/test_styles.py with direct tests for get_case_color (valid roles, unknown role fallback), get_wire_color (valid and unknown wire types), and DiagramTheme enum completeness. The module is small enough that full coverage is trivial.

findings.summary: scripts/01_run_tests.py is 1520 LOC of critical pipeline logic with zero importers and no test coverage
evidence: Existing findings: 'Untested critical module (1520 LOC, 0 importers) — high blast radius'
The file orchestrates coverage enforcement, test suite selection, and pass/fail logic — failures here silently skip coverage checks or misreport results
Contains 5 monster functions (>150 LOC each) with complex branching that is invisible to the test suite
findings.suggestion: Extract the core logic (test discovery, coverage threshold enforcement, result aggregation) into infrastructure/core/ or a dedicated module under infrastructure/reporting/, then write unit tests against those functions. The script itself becomes a thin CLI wrapper.

findings.summary: projects/code_project/scripts/optimization_analysis.py is 1662 LOC with 20 top-level functions and zero test coverage
evidence: Existing findings: 'Untested critical module (1662 LOC, 0 importers) — high blast radius', 'Mixed responsibilities: 20 top-level funcs across 2 disconnected clusters'
The file violates the thin orchestrator pattern — 20 functions in a script file means business logic has leaked into the script layer
Analysis and benchmarking functions cannot be tested in isolation because they live in a zero-importer script, not in projects/code_project/src/
findings.suggestion: Extract analysis logic into projects/code_project/src/ modules (following the thin orchestrator pattern documented in CLAUDE.md), then test those modules directly. The script should be reduced to I/O coordination and plotting.

findings.summary: projects/cognitive_case_diagrams/src/visualization/category_diagram.py is 787 LOC with 2 importers but flagged as undertested
evidence: Existing findings: 'test_coverage: Untested module (787 LOC, 2 importers)'

2 monster functions (>150 LOC each) contain dict key writes that are never read (arg_labels dict at line 363 has keys A, P, S written but never used), indicating dead code paths that tests do not exercise
The module is imported by convenience.py and __init__.py, making it a load-bearing dependency without test verification
findings.suggestion: Add test coverage for the two long functions' core diagram-generation behavior. The dead arg_labels dict is a separate correctness bug — either remove it or use the values; tests would have caught this.

findings.summary: infrastructure/core imports from both infrastructure/project and infrastructure/reporting, inverting the intended dependency direction
evidence: infrastructure/core/multi_project.py:19 imports from infrastructure.project.discovery
infrastructure/core/file_operations.py:114 has a deferred import from infrastructure.project.discovery inside a function body
infrastructure/core/multi_project.py:278 has a deferred import from infrastructure.reporting inside a function body

coupling_matrix shows infrastructure/core/ is the most-imported package (34 inbound from scripts, 32 from tests, 21 from validation, 16 from rendering, etc.) yet it also reaches into project/ and reporting/
holistic_context.architecture shows infrastructure/core imports_from_dirs includes infrastructure/project/ (3 edges) and infrastructure/reporting/ (1 edge)
findings.suggestion: infrastructure/core must not import from infrastructure/project or infrastructure/reporting. Move multi-project coordination logic out of core/ into a dedicated orchestration module (e.g. infrastructure/orchestration/), or push the dependency direction so reporting and project layers import from core only.

findings.summary: Deferred function-level imports used as circular-import workarounds in multiple core modules, obscuring real dependency cycles
evidence: pipeline.py line 457: `from infrastructure.core.file_operations import clean_output_directories` inside _run_clean_outputs method body instead of module top
pipeline.py line 135: `import sys` inside an exception handler, a classic sign of circular-import avoidance
multi_project.py line 278: `from infrastructure.reporting import generate_multi_project_report` deferred inside method
file_operations.py line 114: `from infrastructure.project.discovery import discover_projects` deferred inside function
llm/core/client.py existing_findings lists 6 function-level imports flagged as 'possible circular import workaround'
rendering/core.py line 51: `from infrastructure.core.exceptions import TemplateError` deferred inside render_all method
findings.suggestion: Resolve the real dependency cycles instead of hiding them with deferred imports. Move shared types (TemplateError, file operation helpers) to a lower-level module that neither side imports from. Deferred imports should be reserved for optional heavy dependencies (e.g. matplotlib), not structural workarounds.

findings.summary: infrastructure/__init__.py re-exports symbols that do not exist in infrastructure/core/__init__.py, creating a broken public surface
evidence: infrastructure/__init__.py lines 26-36 attempt to import BuildError, ConfigurationError, TemplateError, load_config, log_timing, setup_logger from .core
infrastructure/core/__init__.py __all__ does not export BuildError, ConfigurationError, load_config, or setup_logger
The entire convenience import block is wrapped in a try/except ImportError with a warnings.warn fallback, confirming it regularly fails

Similarly infrastructure/__init__.py exports extract_publication_metadata, publish_to_zenodo from .publishing but these originate two layers down (metadata.py, platforms.py), bypassing the subpackage boundary
findings.suggestion: Either fix infrastructure/core/__init__.py to export the symbols advertised in infrastructure/__init__.py, or remove the top-level convenience layer entirely. The try/except pattern masks real breakage; callers should import from the authoritative submodule directly.

findings.summary: pipeline.py contains a comment saying stage methods are 'placeholders' when they are fully implemented, misleading future contributors
evidence: Lines 447-449: '# Stage execution methods - these will call the actual scripts/commands\n# For now, these are placeholders that will be implemented to call\n# the existing bash functions or Python equivalents'
All stage methods (_run_setup_environment, _run_project_tests, _run_analysis, etc.) are fully implemented and delegate to _run_script
This comment predates the current implementation and was never removed
findings.suggestion: Remove the obsolete placeholder comment block. The methods are real implementations, not stubs; the comment creates false expectations about the module's state.

findings.summary: validate_markdown_cli.py implements substantial validation business logic directly in the script, violating the thin orchestrator pattern it declares
evidence: File declares itself '# Markdown validation script - THIN ORCHESTRATOR' and 'All business logic is in infrastructure/markdown_validator.py'
Lines 56-182 implement find_markdown_files, collect_symbols, validate_images, validate_refs, validate_math directly in the script module
These functions open files, parse content with regex, walk directories — all substantive business logic
The comment 'Re-export functions from infrastructure for tests' explains the intent but the implementation is not a re-export; it is a duplicate with divergent logic
findings.suggestion: Move find_markdown_files, collect_symbols, validate_images, validate_refs, and validate_math into infrastructure/validation/markdown_validator.py. The CLI script should only call and report, matching the stated architecture contract.

findings.summary: get_config_as_env_vars is a deprecated shim kept in the public API with a live caller, indicating the rename was never completed
evidence: config_loader.py line 265-267: get_config_as_env_vars documented as 'Deprecated: use get_config_as_dict(respect_existing=...) instead' but still exported
config_cli.py line 37 and 82 imports and calls get_config_as_env_vars
Tests in test_config_loader.py still exercise get_config_as_env_vars directly (lines 19, 227, 240, 256)
findings.suggestion: Complete the migration: update config_cli.py and test_config_loader.py to call get_config_as_dict directly, then remove get_config_as_env_vars. Leaving deprecated shims in the public API signals incomplete refactors.

findings.summary: Exit code 2 (graceful skip) is handled inconsistently across pipeline execution paths, with the meaning encoded in three separate places
evidence: _run_script has allow_skip_code parameter that converts returncode 2 to True (line 614)
_resume_pipeline independently re-implements the same exit code 2 conversion logic at lines 397-405, building a replacement PipelineStageResult by hand
_execute_pipeline does NOT perform the same conversion (lines 198-234), meaning the two execution paths handle skip codes differently
The exit code 2 contract is documented only in inline comments, not in a named constant or enum
findings.suggestion: Define an ExitCode enum (SUCCESS=0, SKIP=2, FAILURE=1). Centralise the skip-as-success promotion inside _execute_stage, passing allow_skip=True per stage rather than duplicating it at the call site and in _resume_pipeline.

findings.summary: infrastructure/reporting/__init__.py defines generate_multi_project_report as a real function with a deferred get_logger import inside, creating an inconsistent seam at the package boundary
evidence: Lines 52-110: generate_multi_project_report is implemented inline in __init__.py rather than delegating to a named module
Line 80: `from infrastructure.core.logging_utils import get_logger` deferred inside the function body
The function is a substantive orchestrator (4 steps, error handling, logging) living in an __init__.py, blurring the boundary between package surface and implementation
All other functions in __init__.py are straightforward re-exports from submodules; this one is an outlier
findings.suggestion: Move generate_multi_project_report into a dedicated module (e.g. infrastructure/reporting/multi_project.py) and re-export it from __init__.py. Pull the logger import to module level.

findings.summary: render_all in RenderManager has a nested try/except inside an except block that re-invokes the renderer it just failed to run, as an error-path PDF size check
evidence: Lines 81-96: the outer try calls self.render_slides(source_file, format='beamer'); on exception, the except block contains another try that calls self.slides_renderer.render(source_file, format='beamer') again
The inner call duplicates the failing outer call to inspect the output file size; if the first call failed, the second call likely fails too (silently suppressed by bare except: pass)
This pattern exists to enrich the error message but adds complexity and a hidden second failure path
findings.suggestion: Separate the PDF size check from the render call. If the render raises, inspect the expected output path directly (compute it from the config without invoking the renderer again). Remove the nested try/except.

findings.summary: dashboard_generator.py calls matplotlib.use('Agg') at module import time, creating a global side effect that can break callers who set their own backend
evidence: Line 16: `matplotlib.use('Agg')` executed unconditionally at module level, before any function is called

This is a global matplotlib state mutation; if any other module in the same process imports matplotlib before this and sets a different backend, calling matplotlib.use() after a backend is already loaded raises a warning or silently fails
The MPLBACKEND env var is documented in CLAUDE.md as the standard way to set headless mode; this side effect bypasses that contract
findings.suggestion: Remove the module-level matplotlib.use() call. Instead, set MPLBACKEND=Agg in the pipeline environment (already done in _run_script) and let matplotlib pick it up. If a fallback is needed, call matplotlib.use() only inside a function that creates figures, guarded by `if matplotlib.get_backend() != 'Agg'`.

findings.summary: LLMClient.query() and query_raw() each import time as time_module inside the function body, an unnecessary local import repeated across methods
evidence: client.py line 181: `import time as time_module` inside query()
client.py line 292: `import time as time_module` inside query_raw()
The module already imports `import time` at the top level (line 15); the function-level alias adds noise without purpose
prompt_preview is computed in query() (line 185) and query_raw() (line 295) but existing_findings notes prompt_preview is unused in at least one method
findings.suggestion: Remove the `import time as time_module` lines from both methods; use the already-imported module-level `time` directly. Remove the unused prompt_preview assignment in query().

findings.summary: _setup_log_file_handler catches a bare Exception and falls back to print(stderr) — mixing logging infrastructure with print-based error output
evidence: Lines 131-136: `except Exception as e: import sys; print(f'Warning: could not close log handler: {e}', file=sys.stderr)`
This is the only use of print() in the entire pipeline module; all other error reporting uses logger
Importing sys inside the except block is itself a pattern flagged in the existing findings as 'function-level import (possible circular import workaround)'
findings.suggestion: Replace the print fallback with a direct call to the Python warnings module or to logging.getLogger().warning(). Remove the local sys import by hoisting sys to the module-level imports (it is already imported in other infrastructure modules).

findings.summary: get_testing_config defines _safe_int as a nested function inside the body, creating a private helper that is invisible to tests and has no reuse path
evidence: Lines 445-453: `def _safe_int(key: str) -> Optional[int]:` defined as a closure inside get_testing_config
The nested function is only called three times in the same function body
It captures testing_cfg from the enclosing scope, making it impossible to unit test in isolation and easy to misread as part of the outer function's control flow
findings.suggestion: Extract _safe_int as a module-level private helper with an explicit config_dict parameter. This makes it independently testable and signals that it is an implementation detail of the module, not a transient closure.

findings.summary: validate_markdown_cli.py uses os.path and os.listdir for file operations in a codebase that uniformly uses pathlib
evidence: Line 68: `os.listdir(directory)` to enumerate files
Lines 69-70: `os.path.join(directory, f)` to build paths
Lines 75-80: `os.path.basename(x)` called multiple times in sort key
Line 130: `os.path.join(os.path.dirname(file_path), image_path)` and `os.path.exists(full_path)` in validate_images
Migration signals in holistic_context show 16 remaining os.path usages vs 171 pathlib usages — this file contributes several of them
The module-level import already includes `from pathlib import Path` (line 18) but pathlib is only used for repo_root
findings.suggestion: Replace os.path.join/os.path.basename/os.path.dirname/os.path.exists/os.listdir with pathlib Path equivalents throughout the file, consistent with every other infrastructure module.
